text:
IMPARTIAL NEWS + INTELLIGENT DEBATE

Account

Here's a breakdown of all the key moments from day one of Paris 2024

Team GBsecuredmedals on the opening dayof Olympic competition for the first time since 2004, with a time trial silver for cyclist Anna Henderson following a bronze win for divers Yasmin Harper and Scarlett Mew Jensen in the women’s 3m synchro.

TheParis Olympicsgot underway on Saturday morning after arain-soakedbut eventually spectacularopening ceremonyin front of world leaders the night before that marked theofficial beginningof the 2024 Games, following several days of rugby sevens andfootballqualifiers.

Team GB are said to be aiming for at least 55 medals overall, with 20 of them projected to be gold, following the nation’s success in London, Rio andTokyo– and the diving duo of Harper and Mew Jensen got Britain off to a flying start.

The pair grabbed GB’s first opening day medal since the Athens Games two decades ago, with their bronze also the first women’s diving medal Britain have won at the Olympics in 64 years.

Anna Henderson was the next Brit to get her hands on some silverware, braving slick course conditions that caused several crashes in a rainy women’s road cycling time trial to finish second on her Olympic debut – an achievement made all the more impressive considering she broke her collarbone twice earlier this year.

Buta punctureearly in the men’s time trial may have cost her British cycling teammateJosh Tarlinga first gold medal, with the 20-year-old having to settle for fourth after an enforced change of bikes ate up precious time.

Elsewhere,Adam Peatyensured he’ll be in the top lane for tomorrow night’s 100m breaststroke final with a field-leading time in the semi-finals.

The 29-year-old is bidding to become just the second man in history to win three straight Olympic titles in the same event, following the great Michael Phelps in that feat.

Here are some more highlights from today’s action:

Team GB eventer Tom McEwen says theCharlotte Dujardin horse whipping scandalcame as a shock but vowed to try and change the sport’s image, after a record-breaking day in Versailles.

Dujardin, a three-time Olympic gold medallist in dressage,pulled out of the Olympics on Tuesdayafter video footage emerged of her excessively whipping a horse. The 39-year-old has been provisionally suspended by governing body the FEI and her career lies in tatters – but the saga has not had an immediate impact on Team GB’s equestrian ambitions in Paris.

McEwen was part of a team that set two Olympic records on the opening day to move into an early pole position for eventing gold – an event that consists of dressage, cross-country and show jumping over three days

Laura Collett set a new Olympic record of 17.50 on London 52 to take an early lead in the individual standings, with Ros Canter sitting sixth and McEwen 11th, while GB’s final day score of 66.70 is the lowest-ever team total in Olympic history.

McEwen and Collett were part of Team GB’s gold-medal-winning Tokyo team and McEwen knows how important another gold will be to help move attention away from Dujardin.

“I think it was a shock for all of us, but at the end of it we’re here to portray our sport in a positive light,” he said. “I believe eventing is one of the greatest sports – we’re the triathlon of the horse world.

“I would be more than happy to let anyone come into our yard and see how well those horses are looked after. I think our sport is amazing and these horses are treated like kings and queens.

“I do believe that this week, all of us here can show the sport to be the amazing sport that it is.”

Elsewhere on day one, Gareth Furlong revelled in a dream Olympic debut after helping Team GB’s men begin their hockey campaign with a 4-0 win over Spain.

Furlong, 32, scored twice with trademark drag flicks from penalty corners as Paul Revington’s side delivered a statement of intent in Pool A.

Nick Park’s superb solo strike set the tone on 13 minutes before Furlong’s double, with Rupert Shipperley’s close-range finish adding gloss to the scoreline late on.

“Scoring is part of my job in the team and it’s nice to have the homework pay off,” said Furlong, who was drafted into the GB set-up earlier this year having earned almost 150 Wales caps.

“To come out first game and start with three points is the most important thing.”

In the gymnastics arena, Max Whitlock admitted he had rarely felt so anxious as he helped teammates Joe Fraser, Jake Jarman, Harry Hepworth and Luke Whitehouse all but secure their place in Monday’s team artistic gymnastics final.

Whitlock is calling time on his career here in Paris and knew one slip could have brought the curtain down prematurely at the Bercy Arena.

However, he had nothing to worry about. Jarman, Hepworth and Whitehouse, newcomers at this level, looked totally at home under the glare of the big lights, while Fraser was a reliable as ever.

“There is just a lot of relief right now, qualifications are always so tough, especially at the Olympics, and everybody was really feeling it, we knew what was at stake,” he said.

Elsewhere, Adam Burgess qualified second fastest for Monday’s canoe slalom C1 semi-final, and kayaker Kim Woods secured her place in the women’s K1 semi-final.

Britain’s rowers also booked their place in the men’s and women’s quadruple sculls finals, while Mathildas Hodgkins Byrne and Becky Wilde eased through to the double sculls semis.

And finally, the Olympic hosts France won their first gold medal at Paris 2024 with victory in the men’s rugby sevens final on Saturday evening.

The French sealed the deal with a resounding 28-7 win over defending champions Fiji at the Stade de France, powered on as they had been throughout the tournament by the magic of star playerAntoine Dupont.

Watch every moment of the Olympic Games Paris 2024 live only ondiscovery+, the streaming home of the Olympics

title: What happened on Saturday at the Olympics and which Team GB athletes won medals
author: Alex Dakers, Kat Lucas, Sportsbeat
description: Here's a breakdown of all the key moments from day one of Paris 2024
